data.title: Annaly signals 13% annualized economic return outlook with diversified housing finance model
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Annaly Capital Management (NLY) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Finkelstein highlighted an economic return of 8.1% for the third quarter and 11.5% year-to-date, noting this as "a positive economic return for 8 consecutive quarters, exhibiting the benefits of Annaly's diversified housing finance strategy." He reported the company "raised $1.1 billion of accretive equity in Q3, including $800 million through our ATM program" and reopened the mortgage REIT preferred market with the first residential mREIT issuance in multiple years.
  • Finkelstein emphasized the Agency portfolio increased to just over $87 billion, up 10% quarter-over-quarter, driven by capital deployment in Agency MBS. The company shifted purchases to specified pools in 5.5% and 6% coupons, citing flexibility and risk mitigation.
  • He stated, "Our Onslow Bay platform closed 8 transactions for $3.9 billion in the quarter, generating $473 million of high-yielding OBX retained securities for Annaly and our joint venture," and described Annaly as "the largest nonbank issuer in the residential credit market but a top 10 issuer worldwide of asset-backed and mortgage-backed securities."
  • Finkelstein reported the MSR portfolio increased by $215 million to $3.5 billion, and announced a new partnership with PennyMac Financial Services post-quarter, involving the purchase of $12 billion of low note rate MSR.
  • CFO Serena Wolfe stated, "As of September 30, 2025, our book value per share increased 4.3% from $18.45 in the prior quarter to $19.25. After accounting for our dividend of $0.70, we achieved an economic return of 8.1% for Q3."
  • Wolfe added, "Earnings available for distribution per share for the quarter were consistent with Q2 at $0.73 per share and again exceeded our dividend for the quarter."

Outlook

  • Finkelstein said, "Our investment strategies are well positioned for the balance of the year given declining macro volatility, additional Fed cuts expected and healthy fixed income demand."
  • He projected the Agency sector remains "compelling as spread compression has been achieved through lower volatility and a steeper yield curve."
  • Management expects their Residential Credit business to "further benefit from the growing private label market" and highlighted the MSR portfolio as providing "highly predictable, durable cash flows with limited negative convexity."
  • Finkelstein stated, "this diversified housing finance model has delivered proven results, having generated a 13% annualized economic return over the past 3 years since scaling each business."

Financial Results

  • Wolfe reported, "Book value per share increased 4.3% from $18.45 in the prior quarter to $19.25."
  • Economic return for Q3 was 8.1% and year-to-date economic return reached 11.5%, with positive contributions from all business segments.
  • "Earnings available for distribution per share for the quarter were consistent with Q2 at $0.73 per share and again exceeded our dividend for the quarter," Wolfe said.
  • The Resi Credit business saw "average yields rising to 6.29%," and the net interest spread ex-PAA increased to 1.5%. The net interest margin ex-PAA remained comparable with the prior quarter at 1.7%.
  • Wolfe noted, "Q3 reported weighted average repo days maintained a healthy position of 49 days" with an economic leverage ratio of 5.7x, slightly lower than the prior quarter.
  • Unencumbered assets grew to $7.4 billion, including $5.9 billion in cash and unencumbered Agency MBS.
  • The efficiency ratio improved to 1.41% for the quarter, with an OpEx-to-equity ratio of 1.34%.

Q&A

  • Bose George, KBW: Asked about Agency returns and investment preferences. Finkelstein responded that "Agency still looks very attractive" and the company remains overweight in Agency but aims to raise Resi and MSR to a combined 40%.
  • George inquired about MSR supply and pricing. Ken Adler stated, "The bulk supply has been coming from large participants. Several of them have not previously been sellers...Pricing has been relatively stable throughout the year."
  • Douglas Harter, UBS: Queried about OAS returns and swap spread contributions. V.S. Srinivasan detailed, "the spread to swaps versus treasuries is running around 35 to 40 basis points" and described a blended yield of about 160 basis points.
  • Harter also requested an update on book value. Finkelstein replied, "as of last night, book pre-dividend accrual was up in upwards of 1%. And if you add the dividend accrual, 1.5% to 2% economic return."
  • Harsh Hemnani, Green Street: Asked about rotation into higher coupon specified pools. Srinivasan explained, "the big advantage of specified pools are these are options that we own for a very long time."
  • Jason Weaver, JonesTrading: Asked about lock volume and securitization pace. Michael Fania indicated that "non-QM continues to make progress in terms of market penetration...we actually had our most active month in September."
  • Weaver also questioned the Fed's potential policy tool shift. Finkelstein noted, "the Fed has to evolve as the market evolves...the Fed funds market is just not as good of a barometer of financing rates as repo is."
  • Other analysts queried about duration risk, GSEs as buyers, NII stability, risk appetite, dividend coverage, prepay responsiveness, and return sensitivity. Management consistently emphasized stability, prudent risk management, and a positive outlook for income and dividends.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised recurring questions around risk allocation, portfolio returns, dividend security, MSR supply, and market technicals, demonstrating a slightly positive and constructive tone.
  • Management maintained a confident and pragmatic stance in both prepared remarks and Q&A, with Finkelstein repeatedly affirming, "we feel good about the market...our risk view is not more negative at all by any stretch."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted slightly more positive, particularly around capital raising, portfolio growth, and expectations for continued strong results. Analyst tone remained engaged and pragmatic, with a focus on detailed operational topics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from cautious optimism in Q2 to increased confidence in Q3, with Finkelstein highlighting positive economic returns for eight consecutive quarters and an increase in book value.
  • Strategic focus evolved from a predominantly Agency overweight position in Q2 to a growing emphasis on scaling Resi and MSR to a combined 40% of capital allocation.
  • Key metrics improved: book value per share rose from $18.45 to $19.25, economic return increased from 0.7% in Q2 to 8.1% in Q3, and capital raised was significantly higher.
  • Management’s confidence in dividend coverage and portfolio stability was reiterated, with more explicit references to durable earnings and risk-adjusted returns.
  • Analyst focus remained on risk, asset allocation, and dividend safety, but questions reflected growing interest in the drivers of portfolio expansion and new partnerships.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Finkelstein flagged potential for "further depreciation in the winter seasonals as available-for-sale inventory has increased," but expects cumulative depreciation to be modest.
  • Management continues to stress high credit quality and disciplined underwriting in response to a softer housing market.
  • The company highlighted historically low leverage and significant liquidity as mitigants to market uncertainty.
  • No acute new risks were raised by analysts or management; challenges remain largely macroeconomic and related to housing affordability and rate volatility.

Final Takeaway

Annaly’s third quarter call underscored the company’s consistent economic returns, growing book value, and ability to out-earn its dividend across business segments. Management's increased confidence is reflected in capital deployment, the scaling of Agency, Residential Credit, and MSR portfolios, and a robust liquidity position. Annaly signaled a continued focus on risk-adjusted returns and dividend security, supported by ongoing investments in platform efficiency and strategic partnerships, positioning the company for resilient results into year-end and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Citi initiates coverage of 20 fintechs, IT services providers
data.description:
Citi Research launched coverage on 20 payment processors and IT services providers with Buy and Neutral ratings.

Below are the stocks that were newly rated:

  • Visa (V) (VISA:CA) - Buy
  • Mastercard (MA) (MA:CA) - Buy
  • Block (XYZ) - Buy
  • Toast (TOST) - Buy
  • Affirm Holdings (AFRM) - Buy
  • Global Payments (GPN) - Buy
  • BILL Holdings (BILL) - Buy
  • PayPal (PYPL) (PYPL:CA) - Neutral
  • Fiserv (FI) - Neutral
  • Fidelity National Information Services (FIS) - Neutral
  • Flywire (FLYW) - Neutral
  • Marqeta (MQ) - Neutral
  • Automatic Data Processing (ADP) - Neutral
  • Paychex (PAYX) - Neutral
  • Western Union (WU) - Neutral
  • Accenture (ACN) - Neutral
  • Cognizant Technology Solutions (CTSH) - Neutral
  • EPAM Systems (EPAM) - Neutral
  • Globant (GLOB) - Neutral
  • Genpact (G) - Neutral

"Fintech and IT Service industries have been under pressure given uncertain outlooks, although we see investing opportunities in secular growth winners or companies with a clear accelerating organic growth picture on the horizon," said analyst Bryan Keane.

"In Fintech, the consumer has remained surprisingly resilient despite macro uncertainty with hopes of better clarity creating a potential uptick in transaction volumes over the mid-term while BNPL continues to gain market share, growing well above industry averages," said Keane.

"In IT Services, demand continues to be tepid while GenAI is disrupting traditional business models, causing a sea of change in the industry as pricing moves from headcount based to non-FTE models," added the research note.

data.title: Oceaneering anticipates $390M–$440M EBITDA for 2026 while strengthening ADTech and SSR growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Oceaneering International (OII) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Roderick Larson, President, CEO & Director, highlighted that "we surpassed the high end of our guidance range, generating consolidated adjusted EBITDA of $111 million marking our highest quarterly performance since the fourth quarter of 2015." He attributed this to high-quality backlog conversion in manufactured products, robust Offshore Projects Group (OPG) activity, progress in Aerospace and Defense Technologies (ADTech), and sustained remotely operated vehicle (ROV) pricing and performance.
  • Oceaneering reported revenue of $743 million, up 9% year-over-year, and operating income of $86.5 million, a 21% rise. Free cash flow reached $77 million, and share repurchases totaled approximately $10 million, ending with $506 million in cash.
  • The CEO noted a significant $180 million Subsea Robotics (SSR) contract with Petrobras and emphasized robust growth prospects in Brazil, stating the company is "well positioned" in that market and sees "as big opportunities we've probably ever seen in Brazil."
  • ADTech operating income increased by 36% to $16.6 million on a 27% revenue rise, with margin improvement, driven by defense contract wins. Management noted the segment is expected to grow as a portion of the business over the next 3 to 5 years due to low capital intensity and increasing international demand.
  • Larson announced the planned retirement of CFO Alan Curtis effective January 1, transitioning to an advisory role, and introduced Mike Sumruld as the incoming Senior VP of Finance.
  • Alan Curtis, Senior VP & CFO, stated: "It was -- we had management meetings last week and just to see the whole team rallying around this growth aspect of ADTech and all of the people from the energy side of the business. It was just nice to see 80 people sit there and rally around how can we get there faster."

Outlook

  • For Q4 2025, revenue is expected to be lower, with consolidated EBITDA projected between $80 million and $90 million. Improvements in ADTech and SSR will only partially offset a reduction in international OPG projects.
  • Full year 2025 adjusted EBITDA guidance remains at $391 million to $401 million, with free cash flow of $110 million to $130 million.
  • Initial 2026 guidance forecasts consolidated EBITDA in the range of $390 million to $440 million, with expectations for similar free cash flow as 2025. Growth is projected in ADTech along with stable activity in energy-focused businesses. Management cited expectations for "significant growth in ADTech and stable activity levels across our energy-focused businesses."
  • Oceaneering plans continued share repurchases in 2026, with 5.8 million shares remaining under authorization.

Financial Results

  • The company achieved consolidated adjusted EBITDA of $111 million, the highest since Q4 2015, and revenue of $743 million for Q3 2025.
  • Manufactured Products operating income doubled to $24.7 million on a 9% revenue increase, driven by higher-margin backlog execution and pricing improvements in Grayloc and Rotator product lines. The segment reported a $208 million order intake with a backlog of $568 million and a book-to-bill ratio of 0.82.
  • SSR revenue and operating income were flat, while ROV revenue per day utilized increased to $11,254, offsetting slightly lower utilization rates.
  • OPG operating income rose 17% to $23.7 million on a 16% revenue increase, with healthy vessel utilization. ADTech operating income grew 36% to $16.6 million, with a 27% revenue increase.
  • IMDS operating income and margin improved due to the absence of a prior noncash charge, despite a slight revenue decline.

Q&A

  • Joshua Jayne, Daniel Energy Partners, LLC, asked about Ocean Intervention II and the efficiencies from simultaneous autonomous survey operations. Larson responded that the vessel enables "more with less," reducing costs and improving data quality, resulting in a "more robust solution" for customers.
  • Jayne questioned the company's Brazilian market outlook and SSR contract wins. Larson explained that Brazil's market is "really robust" with significant exploration and infrastructure opportunities, and that "market share continues to increase" as Brazilian customers are first adopters of new technologies.
  • Jayne inquired about ADTech's capital competition and future business share. Larson emphasized the "low capital intensity" and alignment with existing business units, foreseeing "more international opportunities" and growth supported by NATO and allied defense spending. Curtis added that management is rallying around ADTech's growth.
  • Scott Gruber, Citigroup Inc., Research Division, questioned Q4 revenue declines in Manufactured Products and margin implications. Curtis clarified that the segment's earnings quality is improving due to higher-margin backlog conversion and operational excellence, with more benefit expected in 2026. Larson added that the sales funnel is strong and projects are booking into 2027.
  • Gruber asked about ADTech growth cadence embedded in 2026 guidance. Curtis explained the ramp-up of a large-scale project will drive continued revenue progression through 2026 and into 2027, with further opportunities pending.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on operational efficiency, segment growth, market share, and margin sustainability. Questions were constructive, seeking detail on segment dynamics and future growth drivers.
  • Management's tone was confident and forward-looking during prepared remarks and the Q&A, using phrases like "we continue to see growth opportunities" and "we are able to scale that up significantly." Curtis described the team as "rallying around" growth initiatives.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence increased, particularly regarding ADTech and robust market conditions in Brazil. Analyst tone shifted from cautious to more inquisitive about growth and profitability drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management guidance remains consistent, with a narrowed and reaffirmed full-year EBITDA range for 2025. The 2026 outlook was initiated, emphasizing ADTech growth and stable energy business activity, contrasting with the previous quarter's focus on manufacturing and OPG contract visibility.
  • Segment performance highlights shifted from concerns over offshore activity to confidence in backlog conversion and margin expansion.
  • Analyst focus in Q3 shifted towards future growth, capital efficiency, and international market opportunities, especially in Brazil and defense.
  • Management's tone is more optimistic, particularly about ADTech and SSR, compared to a more measured outlook in Q2. Analyst sentiment shifted from cautious (regarding offshore "white space" and order trends) to more growth-oriented inquiries.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified a projected decline in OPG revenue and operating income for Q4 2025 due to the absence of large-scale international projects and lower vessel activity.
  • IMDS is expected to see decreased revenue and operating income in Q4 due to lower activity.
  • The company does not plan to renew a charter for an international vessel, anticipating seasonally lower activity.
  • 2026 guidance assumes the resolution of the government shutdown in 2025, and OPG customer schedules have not yet finalized for some projects, representing a risk to revenue timing.

Final Takeaway

Oceaneering International closed Q3 2025 with its strongest adjusted EBITDA in a decade, underpinned by manufacturing backlog conversion, robust SSR and ADTech performance, and increased free cash flow. The company reaffirmed its full-year guidance and introduced a 2026 EBITDA target of $390 million to $440 million, highlighting significant ADTech growth and stable energy markets. With management transitions underway and continued share repurchases planned, Oceaneering sees expanding opportunities in defense and international energy, while proactively addressing segment-specific risks and cost controls to drive long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Msc industrial targets 3.5%-4.5% daily sales growth in Q1 2026 as leadership transitions and pricing actions drive outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MSC Industrial Direct Co., Inc. (MSM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Erik Gershwind highlighted progress on the company's three top priorities: "maintain momentum in our high-touch solutions, to reenergize our core customer and to optimize our cost to serve." He noted, "we're making strides on all 3 fronts and are doing so in the face of an uncertain environment." Gershwind confirmed daily sales growth returned and operating margin expansion is poised to resume.

  • Gershwind reported, "our high-touch solutions, including vending and implant, continue the strong track record that we've seen all year long." He added that the core customer average daily sales growth rate turned positive, attributing this to initiatives such as website upgrades and marketing efforts, saying, "Since that time, we've seen a steady improvement in core customer performance."

  • Gershwind addressed gross margin pressures: "gross margin came in below our expectations at 40.4%, declining 60 basis points both year-over-year and sequentially. The primary driver here was tariff-driven purchase cost escalation, which came in faster and hotter than we expected during July and August." He stated, "We have since taken action with pricing moves in the fiscal first quarter and have begun to see gross margins improve."

  • Gregory Clark, Interim CFO, reported, "Average daily sales declined 1.3% year-over-year, primarily due to softer volumes in the first half of the fiscal year and a slight FX headwind." Clark added, "We continue to maintain a healthy balance sheet with net debt of approximately $430 million, representing roughly 1.1x EBITDA."

  • Martina McIsaac, President & COO, stated, "During the fiscal fourth quarter, we were most pleased by the return to growth of the core customer with daily sales improving 4.1% year-over-year, driven by both price and volume." She also announced leadership updates, welcoming Jahida Nadi as SVP of Sales and Kim Shacklett as SVP Customer Experience, and noted, "our search for a permanent CFO is underway."

  • Gershwind announced his transition from CEO to Non-Executive Vice Chair of the Board, with McIsaac succeeding him as CEO on January 1.

Outlook

  • McIsaac outlined, "We expect average daily sales to grow 3.5% to 4.5% year-over-year" for fiscal Q1 2026, with the range dependent on the duration of the government shutdown. "We expect adjusted operating margin to fall within the range of 8.0% to 8.6%, which takes into consideration...gross margin to improve from 4Q levels and to be 40.7%, plus or minus 20 basis points."

  • For the full year, expectations include depreciation and amortization costs of $95 million to $100 million, interest and other expense of roughly $35 million, capital expenditures of $100 million to $110 million, and a tax rate between 24.5% and 25.5%.

  • McIsaac projected, "Free cash flow is expected to be approximately 90% of net income and lower than the previous year, driven by working capital needs to support top line growth."

Financial Results

  • Gershwind stated, "average daily sales performed better than expected and improved 2.7% year-over-year."

  • Reported operating margin for the quarter was 8.6%. Adjusted operating margin was 9.2%, which "exceed[ed] the high end of our outlook by 20 basis points" according to Gershwind.

  • GAAP EPS for the quarter was $1.01 compared to $0.99 in the prior year's quarter. Adjusted EPS grew nearly 6%, coming in at $1.09 compared to $1.03.

  • Clark reported free cash flow of $58 million for the quarter, representing 104% of net income, with a full-year free cash flow conversion of 122%.

  • The company returned $229 million to shareholders in the fiscal year through share repurchases and dividends.

Q&A

  • Ryan Merkel, William Blair: Asked about the gross margin impact from supplier price increases and the company's pricing response. Gershwind responded that the surge in supplier increases was "unusual" and McIsaac detailed, "between mid-June...and the end of August, in those weeks, we took more inflation than we took in 9 months post COVID in 2022." She added, "We have now since taken...the right actions in Q1. Gross margin is restoring."

  • Merkel also inquired about gross margin and SG&A expectations. McIsaac said, "We expect to be price cost stable over the cycle and stable through the rest of the year," with incremental margins in the teens in Q1, building through the year.

  • Thomas Moll, Stephens: Asked about seller effectiveness KPIs and operational improvements. McIsaac replied, "We're in about the third inning" of sales process improvements, adding, "We've taken the first step, which is really around good territory design."

  • Moll questioned macro influences versus self-help in results. Gershwind replied, "I wouldn't necessarily call things firming up, but we have pockets and end markets...that we would refer to as stabilizing." He credited core customer improvements primarily to internal initiatives.

  • Christopher Dankert, Loop Capital: Asked about pricing outlook for 2026. McIsaac replied, "I think it's still uncertain. I don't know that I can give you a good answer on that."

  • Katie Fleischer, KeyBanc: Asked about risk of passing supplier cost increases to customers. McIsaac said, "We've been really pleased with the price realization...I'm not worried that we're going to be able to pass it on." Gershwind added that price increases tied to tariffs and raw materials like tungsten could lead to further pricing.

  • Patrick Baumann, JPMorgan: Asked about headcount and marketing spend. McIsaac reported, "our cost structure is too high right now for the size of our business" but noted, "we're actually covering more customers more effectively with fewer people." Gershwind said marketing investment levels will "be fluid based upon the returns that we see."

  • David Manthey, Baird: Asked about direct ship orders and reshoring. Gershwind said direct ship is "the minority" of sales but growing with certain programs, and on reshoring, "we are -- there's tangible data points there...but we're not seeing new greenfield build-out."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism and sought clarity on gross margin stability, pricing power, and operational execution; questions focused on sustainability of improvements and the company's ability to pass on inflation.

  • Management maintained a confident yet measured tone, repeatedly emphasizing preparedness for inflation, productivity initiatives, and flexibility in pricing. McIsaac frequently used phrases indicating confidence about process improvements, while Gershwind acknowledged uncertainty but stressed progress.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone shifted from skepticism about macro softness to a greater focus on execution and self-help; management's tone became more assertive about margin recovery and operational improvements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from stabilization and caution in Q3 to a more assertive outlook for sales growth and margin expansion in Q4 and fiscal 2026.

  • The previous quarter's focus on early green shoots in web and core customer metrics evolved into broader improvements in daily sales and core customer performance, with new highs in vending and implant program growth.

  • Gross margin, previously stable, faced headwinds this quarter but management acted swiftly with pricing in Q1 2026.

  • Leadership transitions and sales team changes marked a notable strategic evolution from Q3 to Q4.

  • Analysts in Q3 were more focused on macro headwinds and pricing, while in Q4 they emphasized the sustainability and execution of internal initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited tariff-driven cost inflation as an ongoing risk, with Gershwind noting, "Tariffs have moved from a possibility to a reality as we're now experiencing meaningful price inflation across many areas of the business."

  • Supplier price increases and raw material inflation, particularly in China-sourced and steel-based products, remain a concern.

  • Government shutdown impacts were highlighted, especially for public sector sales, though management considers these temporary.

  • Headcount and cost structure were acknowledged as areas for continued optimization, with a focus on productivity gains.

Final Takeaway

MSC Industrial Direct enters fiscal 2026 with momentum in sales growth, operational improvements, and a new leadership structure. Management projects daily sales growth in the 3.5% to 4.5% range for Q1 2026, supported by recent pricing actions and productivity initiatives. The company remains focused on margin recovery, cost optimization, and leveraging internal investments to drive value creation and sustained growth, while closely monitoring inflationary pressures and end market conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Quantum computing stocks soar after reports of US mulling stake; government denies
data.description:
Quantum computing stocks surged on Thursday following reports that the Trump administration is negotiating equity stakes in several companies in this space.

However, the government noted that it is not negotiating on equity stakes with quantum computing companies, CNBC reported, citing an administration official.

The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Companies including IonQ (NYSE:IONQ), Rigetti Computing (NASDAQ:RGTI), and D-Wave Quantum (NYSE:QBTS) are discussing the government becoming a shareholder as part of agreements to get funding earmarked for promising technology companies, the Wall Street Journal had reported.

Shares of D-Wave soared about 22%, Rigetti jumped nearly 16%, while IonQ climbed around 12%. Other quantum computing stocks, such as Arqit Quantum (NASDAQ:ARQQ) rose roughly 9%, while Quantum Corp. (NASDAQ:QMCO) and Quantum Computing (NASDAQ:QUBT) each rose around 10%.

Quantum Computing (NASDAQ:QUBT) and Atom Computing are also considering similar arrangements, the report noted.

Update: The story was updated with a report of the government denying negotiations with quantum computing companies.

data.title: Microsoft shows off Mico, its attempt at 'human-centered AI'
data.description:
Amid a push to make its generative artificial intelligence Copilot app friendlier, Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) unveiled a new character on Thursday, known as Mico.

Mico, which gets its name from combining the words Microsoft and Copilot, is expressive, customizable, and warm, Microsoft said in a blog post. In doing so, the Windows maker is taking aim at partner and competitor OpenAI's (OPENAI) ChatGPT, Google's (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) Gemini and other chatbots. 

“A lot of people are evaluating what a relationship with an AI companion should look like and feel like,” Microsoft AI executive Mustafa Suleyman wrote in the blog post. “In this release we’re adding features to make Copilot more personal and more adaptable to your needs and style, while holding true to our brand values. Copilot is designed to be empathetic and supportive, not sycophantic. It will push back on you sometimes, but always respectfully. This is AI that listens. That learns. That earns your trust.”

Suleyman co-founded Inflection AI along with Karén Simonyan. Both of them joined Microsoft in March 2024, with Suleyman hired to run the tech giant's consumer AI unit. Suleyman previously founded DeepMind, which was bought by Google.

In addition to the Micro update, Microsoft made several updates to Copilot, including deeper integration with Outlook, as well as Google. Copilot is also getting updated in Microsoft's Edge browser. Once it receives a user's permission, Copilot can see and use reasoning on tabs to summarize and compare information and take action, such as booking a hotel.

Other Copilot updates are coming to Windows, Pages and Copilot search, Suleyman added.

The updates are now live in the U.S. and will be rolled out in the U.K., Canada and “beyond” in the next few weeks, Suleyman explained.

data.title: Vivid Seats announces a new price guarantee program
data.description:
Vivid Seats (NASDAQ:SEAT) announced a new pricing program aimed at attracting new customers. The company said its Lowest Price Guarantee initiative underscores its position as the most rewarding and value-driven app for live event fans.

The Lowest Price Guarantee program will allow live event fans to purchase tickets through the Vivid Seats (SEAT) app at what the company said will be the best available price. As a key feature, if a fan finds the same ticket listed at a lower price on a qualifying competitor's site, Vivid Seats (SEAT) will match it. Eligible fans will receive 115% of the price difference in Vivid Seats (SEAT) site credit.

As the official ticketing partner of ESPN, Vivid Seats is rolling out a national integrated marketing campaign for the launch of its Lowest Price Guarantee program with the sports entertainment brand.

Vivid Seats (SEAT) CEO Stan Chia said the launch of the Lowest Price Guarantee doubles down on the company's commitment to strengthening its differentiated value proposition.

Shares of Vivid Seats (SEAT) dipped 1.6% in Thursday trading and are down more than 20% over the last six weeks. Vivid Seats (SEAT) is expected to report earnings during the first week of November.

data.title: UK regulator rules against Apple in lawsuit over App Store commission
data.description:
The U.K.'s Competition Appeal Tribunal ruled against Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) after a trial of a class lawsuit alleging that the company abused its dominant position by charging app developers an "unfair" 30% commission through its App Store.

CAT gave a judgment on a claim brought by Rachael Kent on behalf of about 36 million class members that Apple allegedly infringed some competition laws.

"The Tribunal carried out a notional SSNIP test in relation to Apple's Commission and considered that the current level of Commission is materially above competitive market level and that there are no obvious substitution options for that focal product," CAT said in a summary of the judgment.

The tribunal noted that Apple is dominant in the iOS app distribution services and the iOS in-app payment services markets, having near absolute market power in both. The Tribunal considered that Apple's monopoly position in both markets as well as its contractual restrictions, which result in very high barriers to entry, are strongly indicative of dominance.

"Apple has abused its dominant position by charging excessive and unfair prices in the form of its Commission which it charges developers for iOS app distribution services and iOS in-app payment services," said the tribunal. 

CAT noted that Apple has infringed Chapter II and Article 102 through foreclosing competition on both relevant markets by means of restrictions placed on both of those markets. These restrictions include requiring that iOS apps can only be distributed via the App Store, and requiring that iOS app and in-app purchases must use Apple's payment systems.

The tribunal noted that the Class Representative is entitled to damages for the total amount of both of these overcharges which has been passed on, and is entitled to interest on those damages at a simple rate of 8%.

The lawsuit alleged that it cost consumers about 1.5 billion pounds.

Apple did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

"This ruling overlooks how the App Store helps developers succeed and gives consumers a safe, trusted place to discover apps and securely make payments," said an Apple spokesperson to Reuters.
